coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of AIE-active fluorescent organic nanoparticles through one-pot supramolecular polymerization and their biological imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Dazhuang
Liu, Meiying
Zou, Hui
Huang, Qiang
Huang, Hongye
Tian, Jianwen
Jiang, Ruming
Wen, Yuanqing
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Supramolecular interactions between individual molecules are universal forces in living organisms and have been extensively investigated and utilized for fabrication of multifunctional materials over the past few decades. In this work, we reported a rather facile strategy to fabricate the fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) with red fluorescence and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature through the supramolecular interactions between the β cyclodextrin (β-CD) and adamantine terminating AIE dye. The structures of prepared Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs were characterized by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FTIR) spectroscopy. The size and morphology of the final self-assemblies (named as Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs) was characterized by transmission electronic microscopy (TEM). Results demonstrated that these AIE-active FONs can self-assemble into nanoparticles with excellent dispersity and strong fluorescence due to their AIE feature. Biological evaluation demonstrated that Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs has superior biocompatibility and imaging performance. These features endow the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs good performance in biomedical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent organic nanoparticles
Aggregation-induced emission
Supramolecular polymerization
Biological imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing the synthesis of Ad-terminating AIE dye (Ad-Ph-Ad) and β-CD dimmers (β-CD-PEG). These two constituents could formation of amphiphilies through the renowned host–guest interaction between Ad and β-CD.
The 1H NMR spectra of Ad-Ph-Ad, β-CD-β-CD and Ph-Ad/β-CD.

FT-IR spectra of Ad-Ph-Ad, β-CD-β-CD and Ph-Ad/β-CD. The characteristic absorption peak corresponding to the CN was found in the Ad-Ph-Ad and Ph-Ad-β-CD, indicating the AIE-active dye has successfully integrated into Ph-Ad/β-CD.
TEM images of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs. (Scale bar=500nm).

(A) FL spectra of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs in water. Inset: water dispersion of Ph-Ad/β-CD (left cuvette), the image of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs taken at 365nm UV light (right cuvette). (B) The fluorescent intensity of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs before and after continuously irradiated with a UV lamp at 365nm for 1h.
Cell viability of A549 cells after incubation with Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs at different concentrations for 8 and 24h, respectively.
CLSM images of A549 cells incubated with 40μgmL−1 of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs for 3h. (A) excited with 514nm, (B) bright field, (C) merged image of (A) and (B). Scale bar=20μm.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of AIE-active fluorescent organic nanoparticles through one-pot supramolecular polymerization and their biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, The Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supramolecular interactions between individual molecules are universal forces in living organisms and have been extensively investigated and utilized for fabrication of multifunctional materials over the past few decades. In this work, we reported a rather facile strategy to fabricate the fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) with red fluorescence and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature through the supramolecular interactions between the β cyclodextrin (β-CD) and adamantine terminating AIE dye. The structures of prepared Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs were characterized by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FTIR) spectroscopy. The size and morphology of the final self-assemblies (named as Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs) was characterized by transmission electronic microscopy (TEM). Results demonstrated that these AIE-active FONs can self-assemble into nanoparticles with excellent dispersity and strong fluorescence due to their AIE feature. Biological evaluation demonstrated that Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs has superior biocompatibility and imaging performance. These features endow the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs good performance in biomedical fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence technique enjoys the advantages of high selectivity and sensitivity, low interference, non-invasion, and has widely used in the applications of sensing, imaging and detecting [1–5]. Since the first discovery of semiconductor quantum dots as fluorescent biological label in 1998 [6], a variety of fluorescent nanomaterials based on inorganic and organic constituents have been fabricated and extensively investigated for various biomedical applications. To date, multifarious fluorescent nanoparticles including fluorescent silicon nanoparticles, carbon dots, metal nanocrystals and fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) have been prepared [7–23]. Nevertheless some of them have limited practical biomedical applications as a result of their bothersome disadvantages [24,25]. For example, the application of various inorganic nanoparticles is hampered owing to the heavy metal toxicity and undegradability to living organisms. Therefore, seeking for biocompatible and degradable fluorescent probes is highly desirable. Small organic dyes have emerged as important alternatives by taking advantages of designability, biodegradable potential and biocompatibility. Therefore, the fluorescent probes based on FONs have attracted growing attention for its feasibility to design and degradability. Despite significant progress has been made, it still has some problems in the preparation of highly luminescent FONs based on conventional organic dyes because of the notorious aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. It is known that fluorescence of traditional organic dyes will weaken or even extinguish in the aggregation state.

Significant difference from conventional organic dyes, a class of propeller-like fluorophores are weakly emissive or non-luminescent in dilute solution but display strong fluorescence in their aggregate state or solid state [26]. The intriguing phenomenon, which was defined as aggregation induced emission (AIE) was first reported by Luo et al. in 2001 [26]. Restriction of the intramolecular rotation (RIR) mechanism is identified as the main cause of AIE phenomenon [26–32]. Excited state energy is dissipated by the rotation of the molecule rotor structure when the AIE molecules are completely dissolved in solution, and turns into a nonradiative decay channel, resulting in weak fluorescence. However, rotation of the rotor structure is restricted by space and molecular interactions in aggregation state, the nonradiative decay channel is restrained, contributing to the excited molecules go back to ground state through the radiative channel to lead the fluorescence significantly enhanced. The FONs based on AIE-active dyes have advantages of high light, low toxicity, biodegradability and biocompatibility and have already aroused an extensive interest in academic research [33–53]. The unique AIE-active fluorescent molecules that resistant to ACQ effect could provide another method for fabricating new fluorescence probes with superior performance. Nonetheless, the inherent hydrophobicity of pure AIE dyes in physiological conditions will restrict their actual biological applications. Therefore, transforming the hydrophobic dyes to dissolved or dispersed in aqueous solution is indispensable. A general way to overcome this issue is conjugated with the hydrophobic AIE dyes with hydrophilic components and formation of AIE dye containing amphiphilies, which can self-assemble into nanostructures in aqueous solution. It is therefore the final FONs will emit enhanced luminescence owing to the aggregation of AIE-active dyes in the core of nanoparticles.

Supramolecular chemistry has received a great deal of attention in territory of novel functional materials. The self-assembly of supramolecules is an aggregation of multiple molecules via the nonbonding interactions [54–56]. Therefore, there is a great prospect in application to use the principle of the self-assembly supramolecular to obtain well-organized, controllable structure in host–guest chemistry, bioorganic chemistry et al. Although many strategies have been developed for fabrication of AIE-active FONs, the formation of AIE-active FONs based on supramolecular polymerization has been rarely demonstrated [44,57–60]. In this contribution, the AIE-active FONs were facilely fabricated via the supramolecular polymerization between adamantine-terminating AIE dye (Ad-Ph-Ad) and β cyclodextrin dimmers (β-CD-β-CD) owing to the renowned host–guest interaction between adamantine and β-CD (Scheme 1 ). Thus prepared supramolecular amphiphilies will self-assemble into spherical nanoparticles of which hydrophobic Ph-Ad was aggregated in the interior as a core, hydrophilic extend to water as the shell. To evaluate their biomedical application potential, the cell viability and cell uptake behavior were also investigated.

All the chemicals were commercial available and used with no other purification. Both intermediate products AIE dye (NH2-Ph-NH2) and mono-6-deoxy-6-ethylenediamine-β-CD (EDA-β-CD) were synthesized according to literature methods [61,62]. 1-Adamantanecarboxylic acid chloride, triethylamine (TEA) and 4,4′-oxydiphthalic anhydride were bought from Aladdin Company (Shanghai China). Nicolet 5700 was used to get the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. F-4500 FL spectrophotometer with a slit width of 5nm was used to test fluorescence spectra of the emission of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs in THF or water solution. Moreover, the Hitachi 7650B microscope operated at 80kV was used to measure transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs, the means of preparing the TEM samples was placing a drop of nanoparticle aqueous suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. The Bruker Avance-400 spectrometer was used to record 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra with CDCl3 and D2O as solvents.

The detailed process of preparing the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs is described below. Trolamine (TEA) (0.202g, 2.0mmol) was added as an acid binder to accelerate the thermodynamic balance towards the forward reaction after 1-Adamantoyl chloride (0.398g, 2.0mmol) and Ph-NH2 (0.565g, 1.0mmol) were mixed in anhydrous tetrahydrofuran (THF) (20mL), then stirred the mixed solution for 30min under the environment of ice bath. The product was filtered and further purified washing with water, and dried under vacuum for 24h. Finally, the product Ph-Ad was collected.

The β-CD dimers were synthesized via the ring-open reaction of 4,4′-oxydiphthalic anhydride and EDA-β-CD The detailed procedures were described as bellow, EDA-β-CD (2.354g, 2.0mmol) and 4,4′-oxydiphthalic anhydride (0.31g, 1.0mmol) were added into a 100mL flask with anhydrous tetrahydrofuran (THF) (20mL), then TEA (0.22g, 2.2mmol) was injected dropwisely, then stirring at room temperature for 30min. β-CD-β-CD serves as the hydrophilic host structure and Ad-Ph-Ad serves as the hydrophobic guest structure were mixed with a mole ratio of 1:1, after that they were added in anhydrous dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), then stirred the mixed solution for one hour under room temperature. Then dialyzed the solution with ethyl alcohol, and washed the products with deionized water for three times, as a result, the products Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs were obtained.

On account of the excellent water dispersity, the promising application of the cell imaging of the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs was evaluated by a CLSM Zeiss 710 3-channel (Zeiss, Germany). A549 cells with a density of 1×105 cells per dish were cultured in a culture dish for all night. Next the A549 cells were incubated with a consistent concentration (40μgmL−1) for 3h at 37°C. Then the incubated cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) three times to get rid of the redundant FONs that did not enter the cells. After that, the A549 cells were merged with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min at room temperature. And the fluorescent images were excited with a 514nm laser.

The performance of biocompatibility of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs was improved apparently after the modification of the supramolecules. Cell viability is an important parameter for evaluation of biocompatibility of biomaterials. Thus, the assessment of cell viability was aimed at evaluating the possible biomedical applications of the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs using cell counting kits 8 (CCK-8) assay [63–66]. Firstly, A549 cells were seeded into 96-well microplates at density of 5×104 cellsmL−1 in 160µL of respective media containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). Secondly, the plates were washed with PBS after the cells have been tightly attached. Different concentrations of Ph-Ad/β-CD-PEG FONs with the final concentrations from 10–120μgmL−1 were added to each well and incubated with cells for 8 and 24h. Thirdly, all the cell plates were washed with PBS three times to remove the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs that did not enter the cells. Then, 10µL of CCK-8 dye and 100µL of DMEM cell culture medium were added to each well, and the A549 cells were incubated for another 2h at 37°C. At last, the microplates were analyzed using a microplate reader (VictorIII, Perkin–Elmer). Measurements of formazan dye absorption were carried out at 450nm, and the reference wavelength is 620nm. Owing to the number of the living cells was proportional to the values, compared with the control groups (cells were incubated with complete cell culture medium), the reduction percentage of CCK-8 dye represented 100% WST reduction. Three replicate plates were used for each control group and testing the concentrations of each microplate, which means that the experiments were repeated three times. The survival rate of cells was expressed as absorbance, referring to the control groups. The results were given as mean ± standard deviation (SD).

The chemical structure and morphologies feature of the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs were evidenced by the 1H NMR, FT-IR spectroscopy and TEM. The intermediates were characterized by 1H NMR spectroscopy and gave satisfactory analysis data corresponding to their molecular structures. The 1H NMR spectra of Ph-NH2, Ad-Ph-Ad, β-CD-β-CD and Ph-Ad/β-CD were shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. 1 . As compared with NH2-Ph-NH2, the spectrum of Ad-Ph-Ad displays some new emerging peaks at δ 2.0–1.5 and the disappeared proton resonance at δ 6.69, indicating the successful conjugation of Ad structure. Aside from the peaks of the EDA-β-CD, there were still some characteristic peaks belongs to the resonances of protons of benzene ring in the spectra of β-CD-β-CD, confirming that two moieties were successfully conjugated via ring-open reaction. In the 1H NMR spectrum of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs, we can observe the peaks both Ad-Ph-Ad and β-CD-β-CD moieties, suggesting that Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs were fabricated successfully through host–guest interaction between Ad-Ph-Ad and β-CD-β-CD.

The FT-IR technique was another good method to confirm the chemical structure of the products. Similar results are obtained from the FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. 2 , the strong absorption bands observed at 1661cm−1 both in Ad-Ph-Ad is associated with its –CO stretching vibrations. In addition, the strong absorption bands at 1665cm−1 belongs to –CO stretching vibrations is also observed in the sample of β-CD-β-CD, providing a powerful evidence for the successful formation of Ad-Ph-Ad and β-CD-β-CD. In the sample of Ph-Ad/β-CD, we can observe two strong absorption bands at 2311 and 1055cm−1, which can be assigned to the –CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. In comparison with the characteristic signals of Ad-Ph-Ad in the FT-IR spectrum, the strong absorption bands at 1055cm−1 is new emerged, indicating the existence of β-CD-β-CD structure in fabricated FONs. Similarly, the band at 2311cm−1 is also new emerged compared with β-CD-β-CD. Hence, the appearance of the stretching vibration of –CN and CO in the IR spectrum of Ph-Ad/β-CD indicated that Ad-Ph-Ad and β-CD-β-CD were conjugated successfully through supramolecular interaction in aqueous solution.

Size and morphology of the luminescent nanoparticles (Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs) was characterized by TEM. From the TEM image of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs, we can recognize a large number of the uniform spherical nanoparticles, with the diameter less than 200nm (Fig. 3 ). Besides, the exact size distribution of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs was further detected. As shown in Fig. S5, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment has been carried out to determine the size distribution of FONs dispersed in PBS buffer solution. The results showed the FONs had diameter about 247±102nm with polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.202. Because the supramolecular amphiphilies were obtained via host–guest interaction between hydrophobic AIE-active Ad-Ph-Ad and hydrophilic host β-CD-β-CD, the amphiphilies can self-assemble into spherical nanoparticles in aqueous solution. Based on the properties of Ad-Ph-Ad and β-CD-β-CD, we can speculate that the hydrophobic Ad-Ph-Ad will be aggregated in the interior as the core, while hydrophilic β-CD-β-CD will be extended to water as the shell. It is therefore the final Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs will emit intensive luminescence due to their AIE feature. On the other hand, the hydrophilic β-CD will endow them well water dispersibility.

The fluorescence property is indispensable to evaluate potential biomedical applications of the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs. As indicated by TEM image, the supramolecular amphiphilies will self-assemble into nanoparticles, in which the hydrophilic components of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs will covered on the FONs. Therefore, we can speculate the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs will display excellent water dispersity. As shown in insets of Fig. 4 (A), the FONs were homogeneous dispersed, and no obvious macroscopic aggregates can be observed (left cuvette). Under illumination with a UV lamp at 365nm, the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs emit bright red fluorescence (right cuvette). The UV–Vis spectrum of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs was shown in Fig. S2, it can be seen that many peaks ranging from 250 to 600nm can be found in the spectrum. These absorption peaks can be used for confirming the existence of AIE-active dye in the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs. On the other hand, we also found that the absorption intensity of UV–Vis spectrum was elevated from 800nm. This indicated that the successful formation of nanoparticles after Ph-Ad/β-CD dispersed in water and also evidenced the successful preparation of Ph-Ad/β-CD copolymers through the host–guest interaction. The fluorescent spectra demonstrated that the maximum excitation wavelength was at 505nm, while the maximum emission wavelength located at 600nm. Besides, it is also significant to evaluate the photostability of the fluorescence bioprobes. It was exciting to observe that the fluorescence intensity could remain stable during 1h persistent eradiation with the ultraviolet lamp. Satisfactory result was obtained, with no obvious fluorescence intensity decrease after an hour continuously irradiated. Moreover, the AIE feature of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs was further investigated. The FL spectra of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs in THF and water mixtures with different water fractions (f w) are shown in Fig. S2 (f w =0 to 100%). Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs show moderate fluorescence in THF. With gradual addition of water into THF (f w =0 to 50%), the emission of Ph-Ad/β-CD was observably weakened following the increased solvent polarity, result in generating the transformation into the twisted intramolecular charge transfer state. The fluorescence intensity was enhanced when f w > 50vol%, and was continuous intensified along with the increase in f w, suggesting that Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs are promising AIE-active probes (Fig. S3).

Further information about fluorescence properties of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs were obtained from fluorescence dynamics measurements. The quantum yield of Ad-terminating AIE dye (Ad-Ph-Ad) powders was up to 23.5%. In contrast, the fluorescence quantum yield of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs was decreased to 1.14%. This variation occurred, was probably due to the encapsulation of AIE dye in interior of FONs. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) spectra of Ad-Ph-Ad with respect to Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs were displayed in Fig. S4. The fluorescence decay values of Ad-Ph-Ad and FONs were 2.73ns and 2.43nm, respectively. These results demonstrated the successful formation of FONs of which AIE dye was capsulated in the interior.

The biocompatibility of biomaterials should be first evaluated for biomedical applications. In this work, the cell viability was determined based on the typical CCK-8 assay. The survival rates of cells were expressed as absorbance, so the cell viability values were described the changes of cell survival rates in different time and concentrations. It is apparent that there is no obvious decrease for the survival rate of cells when they were incubated with 10–120 µg mL−1 of the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs for 24 h (Fig. 5 ). Despite the concentration of the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs was even as high as 120mgmL−1, the survival rate of A549 cells remained over 100%. The increase of cell viability values is possibly ascribed to the internalization of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs, which have absorption at the wavelength 450nm. On the other hand, due to the cells were completely washed with PBS. During this procedure, the dead cells were removed after washing. Therefore, the high cell viability values based on CCK-8 assay could reflect the low cytotoxicity of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs. Combined with the high water dispersity, small size and intensive fluorescence, the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs with excellent biocompatibility should be promising candidates for constructing multifunctional platforms for potential biomedical applications.

On account of the excellent biocompatibility, cell uptake behavior of the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs was further evaluated by CLSM observation. After the A549 cells were incubated with 40μgmL−1 of the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs for 3h at 37°C, owing to the smooth dyeing of the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs, it is easy to figure that most of A549 cells were successfully dyed with the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs (Fig. 6 ). Many areas with fluorescent signals were observed by the fluorescent image when cells were irradiated with laser 514nm. Interesting, many dark areas surrounded by these fluorescent signals were also found from the fluorescent image (Fig. 6(A)). The above observation indicated that Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs were internalized by cells and mainly distributed in the cytoplasm. The dark areas at the center should be the locations of cell nuclei. It is reasonable that the Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs cannot enter in cell nuclei because the size of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs is obviously larger than that of the nucleus pore. Fig. 6(B) shows the bright field of A549 after incubation with Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs. It can be seen that the cells still adhered to the cell plates and kept their normal morphology. This also implied the good biocompatibility of Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs. The emerged image of Fig. 6(A) and (B) shows that the areas with fluorescent signals are just covered on the locations of cells and center of these cell locations are absent of fluorescence (Fig. 6(C)). This clearly confirmed that Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs were internalized by cells through nonspecific phagocytosis. To further confirm the FONs were accumulated in cytoplasm of A549 cell, the imaging region of FONs in cells was determined by co-staining cells with both Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs and Hoechst 33258. In contrast, Hoechst 33258 can light the cell nucleus under the excitation wavelength of 405nm. The luminant regions of Hoechst 33258 and FONs were displayed in Fig. S6(A) and (B), respectively. From Fig. S6(A)–(D), it can be evidently identified that most of FONs were accumulated in the cytoplasm of A549 cells and emitted intense red fluorescence upon the excitation of 514nm. The effective dying of cells using Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs indicated that their potential for biological imaging. On the other hand, if other functional components such as targeting agents and drug loading groups etc are integrated into these AIE-active FONs, multifunctional nanotheranostics could be fabricated through the facile supramolecular polymerization.

In summary, we reported for the first time to fabricate the fluorescent probes using Ad-terminating AIEgens as the core, and the hydrophilic and biocompatible β-CD dimer as encapsulation matrix through the facile supramolecular polymerization, which is relied on the host–guest interaction between Ad and β-CD. The successful fabrication of such supramolecular amphiphilies was confirmed by a series of characterization techniques. The final Ph-Ad/β-CD FONs show small size, high water dispersibility, desirable optical properties and biocompatibility. In light of its simplicity and high efficiency as the cell imaging bioprobe, further development of AIE fluorogens with longer wavelength emission will accelerate the development of specific bioprobes for in vivo imaging. In future, such AIE-active FONs with superb dyeing effect, dispersibility and biocompatibility will provide a promising platform to construct multifunctional bioprobes for cell apoptosis and drug screening. Supramolecular polymers endowed with responsiveness to stimuli, self-healing, and degradability, open new avenues for the construction of novel AIE-active probes with intelligent and low in vivo toxicity for potential clinical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2017.05.024.
